BBM1251.jpg: Andrea’s lens caught me near Gallina as the Via Cassia unrolled through dry Tuscan folds. I was a small figure at the road’s edge, my loaded bike dragging its shadow through heat and wheat-colored distance. Behind me, Radicofani’s anger still burned. Ahead of me, San Quirico waited somewhere beyond the pale line, and I remember wanting the photograph to show the road conquering me instead.
_MG_0201-2.jpg: Morning began inside nylon and stale warmth, my sleeping pad pressed against me like proof that I had crossed another night. I woke before the road, before the lake brightened, before gratitude became departure. The tent wall caught a shard of light, and I remember thinking how fragile my shelter was against Italy’s August geography: canvas, dust, insects, breath, and a body trying to become brave.
_MG_0203-2.jpg: Marco and Paola’s tents sat above the lake like temporary houses stitched into weeds. I looked at their camp and felt my own journey briefly become communal, less heroic, more domestic. The shoreline glimmered beyond dense vegetation, while loose clothes and small gear marked the ground between us. Before leaving, I carried their kindness with me, folded inside my bags like another necessary layer.
_MG_0207.jpg: At the lakeside, the yellow boat rested in rough grass, its little Italian flag bright against mud, weeds, and morning haze. I stood there with my bike waiting somewhere behind me, trying to delay the climb that would take me south. The uneven ground, bare soil, wildflowers, and water made the map feel physical again. My route was not a line; it was terrain resisting me.
_MG_0212.jpg: The sign faced the lake like a quiet instruction, while boats lay scattered across the muted water. I had not yet learned how much signage would govern my day: warnings, distances, church doors, hostel rules, invisible credentials. Here the dawn still felt forgiving. The grainy sky and low colors softened me, and my departure from Montefiascone seemed almost gentle before the Cassia hardened under my wheels.
_MG_0218-2.jpg: An orange boat, a bench, a grove, and the lake beyond: the morning offered me a small theater of stillness. I photographed it because I knew my body would soon become all movement, all heat, all calculation. The trees held shade like a promise I could not keep. Behind me were tents and borrowed friendship; before me, the road began widening into distance and doubt.
_MG_0219.jpg: The tents were pushed against foliage rather than placed in any orderly campground, and that suited my memory of the trip. I was always half inside permission, half outside it. Rough ground, dry stems, and small debris surrounded us, but the lake was close enough to soften everything. I packed my things slowly, knowing that once I left, hospitality would again become something I had to negotiate.
_MG_0229-2.jpg: Marco’s workbench looked like a small republic of movement: handlebars, saddle, bags, tools, and a black-orange duffel waiting beside me. I remember saying goodbye with my loaded bicycle already pulling my attention toward the road. The place smelled of repairs and departures. Before noon, kindness still had faces and names; later, after Radicofani, kindness would become rarer, stranger, and more difficult for me to ask for.
_MG_0231-4.jpg: In San Lorenzo Nuovo, I met Giobatta and Siera Mae between bicycles and gelato signs, our hands briefly clasped in the middle of the street. Their road and my road crossed like two drawn lines on warm plaster. I remember his ease, his French horizon, his warning about Lazio traffic. For a moment, someone recognized me not as lost, but as moving with purpose.
_MG_0233 1.jpg: The Cassia stretched ahead through Acquapendente, patched asphalt flickering under my wheel and dark spots floating in the sky like dirt on memory itself. I was learning that the road was never clean: not visually, not emotionally. Trucks, heat, repairs, and doubt stayed with me. Each blemish on the image feels accurate now, because my confidence was already scratched, but still rolling forward.
_MG_0238-2.jpg: The sign ahead sharpened while my bicycle blurred in the foreground, as if the road cared more about direction than about me. I remember the dry vegetation beyond the guardrail and the cracked surface under my tires. Geography became an argument: asphalt, slope, shoulder, sun. My hands held the bars, but the route held me tighter, pulling me toward Radicofani’s hill.
_MG_0240-2.jpg: The Via Cassia opened in front of me, stripped of traffic, edged by white lines, and emptied into heat. My touring bags turned the bicycle into a small animal of burden, faithful but heavy. I trusted the road because there was no alternative. Between San Casciano and the next climb, I felt both free and exposed, my body measured against a landscape too wide for complaint.
_MG_0249-2.jpg: Two warning signs announced three kilometers, but they did not confess how long a hill can become inside my legs. The road to Radicofani rose through yellow grass and sparse shrubs, a dry geometry of punishment. I remember negotiating with myself at every bend. Three kilometers was not distance; it was time, sweat, hunger, pride, and the slow transformation of scenery into resistance.
_MG_0252-2.jpg: The paired signs repeated their warning, and I read them like a prophecy nailed into the roadside. My bike was heavy, my patience thin, and the hills around Radicofani burned straw-gold under the August sun. I kept climbing because stopping would have made the slope larger. The landscape looked emptied of mercy, yet it also gave me the exact shape of my stubbornness.
_MG_0256.jpg: Vineyard rows filled the foreground, disciplined and green against the exhausted hills. I saw the road angle upward and the distant tower on the ridge, and my mood tightened. Radicofani was visible enough to tempt me, far enough to punish me. The cultivated lines beside the asphalt made my wandering feel unruly. I was not planted here; I was passing through, dragging my life behind.
_MG_0259.jpg: Posts and wires held the vines in order while I moved without such support. The hilltop building stood on the horizon, a fixed mark I could neither ignore nor quickly reach. My bike creaked under water, bags, and ambition. I remember envying the vineyard’s rootedness, then resenting it. The road made me temporary, but the land around Radicofani seemed built from centuries of staying.
_MG_0261.jpg: The white edge line ran beside me like a fragile promise, with no center divider to separate desire from danger. I kept close to the shoulder, watching marker posts pass one by one. The road into Radicofani felt exposed, almost surgical, cutting through heat and dry fields. My body narrowed to breathing, pedaling, and measuring the next patch of shade that never arrived.
_MG_0265.jpg: A no-hunting sign stood on its worn post, absurdly official in the open dryness. I photographed it because I was the one being pursued: by heat, by hunger, by the gradient, by my own decision to continue. The sky held a thin cloud like a scratch. Around me, the land was silent, but my body was loud with pulse, thirst, and complaint.
_MG_0268.jpg: The road emptied again, no cars, no people, only reflector posts and a pale line guiding me through late-summer glare. Dark marks in the sky came from the lens, but I now read them as fatigue made visible. My memory of this stretch is mostly sound: chain, breath, tires, wind. The landscape gave me nothing soft, and still I moved across it.
_MG_0269.jpg: Dry grass dominated the slope, and the distant structure sat alone on the horizon like a verdict. I kept looking at it, believing arrival would solve hunger, tiredness, and irritation. My road imagination was naive: every summit promised relief, but each relief demanded another negotiation. The wall, the fields, the hilltop—everything ahead of me seemed still, while I was spending myself to reach it.
_MG_0271.jpg: Radicofani’s tower lifted its crenellated edge above the darker slope, finally close enough to become architecture rather than rumor. I had climbed through straw fields and hard light to see it this clearly. My legs were already bargaining with my anger. The contrast between dry yellow and wooded green felt almost moral, as if the hill kept two climates: one for welcome, one for refusal.
_MG_0279-2.jpg: My helmet flashed blue and white against the golden roadside, a small shell placed in a landscape of heat. I must have stopped to breathe, or to prove to myself that I was still there. The dry vegetation pressed close to the shoulder. Behind me was the climb already earned; ahead of me, the town where fatigue would meet bureaucracy and turn into anger.
_MG_0284.jpg: The road bent gently toward distant houses, handlebars blurred at the bottom of my vision. I remember this as the grammar of arrival: curve, slope, roofline, hope. The bike cockpit filled my foreground because everything else had to be reached through it. My world had narrowed to cables, bags, brakes, asphalt, and the stubborn belief that the next cluster of buildings might finally receive me.
_MG_0294.jpg: A snow-chain sign appeared in August, blue and formal beside the priority-road diamond, and I laughed at the absurdity of cold imagined in that heat. My bicycle blurred below, heavy with summer. The landscape around Radicofani had already scorched me, yet the sign spoke of ice, obligation, winter. It reminded me that roads keep memories of other seasons, while travelers suffer only the present one.
_MG_0298.jpg: In Radicofani, my loaded bicycle leaned against dark stone and red brick bands, finally still after the climb. The facade looked older and tougher than my irritation, as if the town had absorbed centuries of exhausted arrivals. I thought rest would follow. Instead, rules, passports, and refusal waited for me nearby. The geography had brought me up; the institution would push me back down.
_MG_0306-2.jpg: At Piazza San Pietro, the church steps spread in horizontal bands, and my bicycle rested against the right wall like evidence. I had climbed for hours to reach this square and the pilgrim reception near it. When I said I had no credential, welcome narrowed into exclusion. The dark stone facade still feels beautiful to me, but my memory of it is edged with anger.
_MG_0309.jpg: Leaving Radicofani, I entered an empty road with my anger riding beside me. The white reflector post marked the shoulder, but nothing marked the humiliation of being turned away after that climb. I descended toward another possibility, another phone call, another bed that might or might not accept me. The land opened again, and my body converted frustration into speed, hunger, and determination.
_MG_0316-2.jpg: The Cassia’s guardrail ran tight beside my bicycle, leaving only a narrow shoulder between me and traffic. I remember stopping there after Radicofani, still carrying the sting of rejection. Dry grass filled the ditch, and the road pulled south through Castiglione d’Orcia. My bags looked oversized against the metal rail, but they held everything I trusted: tent, mat, camera, clothes, and unfinished courage.
_MG_0317.jpg: My bicycle stood loaded beside the Via Cassia, with the sky marked by faint spots and the landscape stretched dry behind it. I see the image now as a pause after anger, not peace. The road was broad, exposed, and indifferent. I had asked for shelter and received a rule; now my bike and I were again responsible for each other.
_MG_0318-2.jpg: The front wheel turned toward the shoulder, my bicycle leaning into the guardrail as if it too needed support. I remember the roadside vegetation, yellow-green and brittle, matching my mood after Radicofani. Stopping was dangerous because stillness invited doubt. Yet I photographed the machine, the ditch, the rail, the empty edge, and let the geography witness me before I pushed on toward San Quirico.
_MG_0321.jpg: The road across Castiglione d’Orcia looked bleached and open, with harvested fields rolling away from my bicycle. I was hungry by then, thinking of food before scenery, but the landscape kept presenting itself in severe beauty. My bags and strapped cylinder made the bike look prepared, while I felt improvised. The hills did not care. They simply received my shadow and sent it forward.
_MG_0322-2.jpg: A silver cylinder was strapped to my bicycle, absurdly bright against fields the color of old bread. I had become a moving inventory: water, mat, camera, hunger, anger, hope. The terrain around me was wide and almost treeless, the kind of openness that makes distance visible but shelter invisible. I kept photographing because images gave my exhaustion a place to stand outside my body.
_MG_0323-3.jpg: My touring bike occupied the roadside like a small loaded animal, while golden-brown fields spread on both sides of the Cassia. I had left the hilltop behind, but not the feeling of being judged by thresholds. The road’s emptiness gave me back a little dignity. There was no receptionist here, no credential required, only asphalt, heat, and the question of how far I could continue.
_MG_0326-2.jpg: The bicycle’s bags, bottle, and front clutter filled the foreground, while arid mounds and open fields stretched beyond. I remember feeling that my possessions had become my architecture: everything necessary attached to a frame, everything unstable held by straps. The road made a room of distance around me. After Radicofani, the landscape’s indifference felt kinder than human refusal, because it asked only that I keep moving.
_MG_0339-2.jpg: The guardrail doubled itself in pale metal and rusted brown, running parallel to the road through dry fields. I stood there with my bike and felt the day split the same way: kindness and refusal, climb and descent, hunger and relief still deferred. The vegetation was burnt yellow, the horizon low, and my route pressed onward. Geography became a corridor for everything I could not say.
_MG_0341-3.jpg: Near Gallina, the road narrowed past a stone building and a chevron sign, just before Andrea’s camera noticed me. I remember seeing the red car, the professional lens, and turning back to ask for the photograph. My Italian was imperfect but urgent. I wanted proof from outside my own camera that I had existed on that road, small against Tuscany, moving despite fatigue.
